findings.title: userId in CreateTeleworkDto and CreateRecurringRuleDto uses @IsString() instead of @IsUUID()
findings.description: The userId field in CreateTeleworkDto (and identically in CreateRecurringRuleDto) is annotated with @IsString() instead of @IsUUID(). Although the field is optional and the service performs a downstream Prisma lookup that will 404 if the ID does not exist, a non-UUID string accepted by the pipeline will cause Prisma to throw an internal database error (invalid UUID format) rather than a clean NestJS NotFoundException, potentially leaking implementation details. More importantly, the intent of the field is clearly a UUID primary key; @IsString() is under-constrained. All other ID fields in the same codebase (e.g., taskId, projectId in CreateTimeEntryDto) correctly use @IsUUID().
findings.root_cause: Copy-paste pattern chose @IsString() instead of @IsUUID() for optional userId body fields in telework DTOs.
findings.impact: A caller can supply any string (e.g., SQL-like string, overly long string) as userId. Prisma will send a malformed UUID to PostgreSQL, producing a 500 with a Prisma error message rather than a clean 400/404; the stack trace may be visible in development/permissive logging.
acceptance_criteria: POST /api/telework with body { date: '2026-06-01', userId: 'not-a-uuid' } returns HTTP 400 (class-validator error), not HTTP 500.
POST /api/telework/recurring-rules with body { dayOfWeek: 1, startDate: '2026-06-01', userId: 'not-a-uuid' } returns HTTP 400.
findings.verification_command: curl -s -X POST http://localhost:4000/api/telework -H 'Authorization: Bearer <admin_token>' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"date":"2026-06-01","userId":"not-a-uuid"}' | jq '.statusCode'
findings.notes: Same pattern applies to CreateRecurringRuleDto (apps/api/src/telework/dto/create-recurring-rule.dto.ts lines 19-21). UpdateRecurringRuleDto inherits via PartialType so is implicitly affected.

findings.title: Unbounded date range in GET /telework triggers O(days × rules) sequential DB queries with no upper bound
findings.description: The findAll service method calls expandRecurringRulesForRange whenever both startDate and endDate query params are provided. That private method iterates over every calendar day in the range for every active recurring rule, issuing up to two sequential Prisma queries per matching day (findUnique + create). There is no upper bound on the date range accepted from the user-supplied query params. A user with telework:read (any authenticated user who has permission to list telework) can pass startDate=1970-01-01&endDate=2099-12-31, causing ~47,450 loop iterations per rule. With, e.g., 10 active rules each covering one day per week, this translates to ~7,000 sequential DB queries per request, effectively DoS-ing the database connection pool for the duration of the call.
findings.root_cause: expandRecurringRulesForRange is called with unvalidated user-supplied date strings and performs N×M sequential DB I/O with no range cap or batching.
findings.impact: Any user with telework:read permission (minimum CONTRIBUTEUR role) can exhaust the Prisma connection pool and stall all API requests for the duration of the query, causing a denial of service.
findings.suggested_fix:
1. Add a maximum range cap in the controller or service (e.g., 366 days): reject or clamp if endDate - startDate > 366 days.
2. Alternatively, move the recurring-rule expansion to a background job / cron and remove the inline call from findAll.
3. Short-term: add @IsDateString() validation on the query params and validate that endDate >= startDate and the range <= 366 days in the service before calling expandRecurringRulesForRange.
acceptance_criteria: GET /api/telework?startDate=1970-01-01&endDate=2099-12-31 returns HTTP 400 with a message about the date range.
GET /api/telework?startDate=2026-01-01&endDate=2026-12-31 (366 days) completes in under 2 seconds regardless of the number of active recurring rules.
findings.notes: The same issue exists in generateSchedulesFromRules (apps/api/src/telework/telework.service.ts lines 855-942), which is triggered by POST /telework/recurring-rules/generate. That endpoint is guarded by telework:create but has the same unbounded iteration. Also: expandRecurringRulesForRange in findForPlanningOverview (line 246) takes dates from internal callers, not directly from user input, so that path is lower risk.

findings.title: description field in CreateTimeEntryDto lacks @MaxLength constraint
findings.description: The description field in CreateTimeEntryDto has @IsString() and @IsOptional() but no @MaxLength(). The global ValidationPipe has whitelist: true and forbidNonWhitelisted: true but does not impose length constraints. The server body limit is 1 MiB (set in main.ts), which prevents the worst case, but a description field value of ~1 MB is still legal from the validator's perspective and will be persisted verbatim to the database. This is inconsistent with best practices for free-text fields.
findings.root_cause: No @MaxLength decorator was added when the DTO was authored.
findings.impact: Users can store description strings up to ~1 MB (the server body limit), potentially inflating storage and slowing queries. The 1 MiB bodyLimit in main.ts provides a hard stop against the extreme case.
acceptance_criteria: POST /api/time-tracking with description longer than 2000 characters returns HTTP 400.
findings.verification_command: python3 -c "import sys; print('a'*2001)" | xargs -I{} curl -s -X POST http://localhost:4000/api/time-tracking -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"date":"2026-06-01","hours":4,"activityType":"DEVELOPMENT","taskId":"<uuid>","description":"{}"}' | jq '.statusCode'
findings.notes: The 1 MiB bodyLimit (main.ts:98) caps the absolute worst case. Impact is storage/query-performance rather than a direct security exploit.

findings.title: PATCH/DELETE recurring-rules/:id routes lack @OwnershipCheck decorator (rely solely on service-layer check)
findings.description: The PATCH /telework/recurring-rules/:id and DELETE /telework/recurring-rules/:id routes do not carry @OwnershipCheck + OwnershipGuard, unlike the analogous PATCH/:id and DELETE/:id routes for TeleworkSchedule and for TimeEntry (which use @UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard, OwnershipGuard) + @OwnershipCheck). Ownership enforcement for recurring rules is implemented correctly at the service layer (telework.service.ts updateRecurringRule/removeRecurringRule), but there is no declarative guard-level enforcement. This is a defense-in-depth gap: a future refactor could bypass the service check while the lack of guard annotation goes unnoticed.
findings.root_cause: The recurring-rule sub-resource was added after the ownership guard pattern was established for TeleworkSchedule, and the guard decorator was not applied.
findings.impact: No current exploit — the service-layer check is correct and will throw ForbiddenException. The gap becomes exploitable only if the service check is later removed or bypassed via an internal caller.
acceptance_criteria: PATCH /api/telework/recurring-rules/<other-user-rule-id> by a non-admin user returns HTTP 403.
DELETE /api/telework/recurring-rules/<other-user-rule-id> by a non-admin user returns HTTP 403.
findings.verification_command: N/A — manual verification (service-level check already enforces this; guard absence is a defense-in-depth gap)
findings.notes: The service-level check is correct; this is defense-in-depth only.

verified_clean: No raw SQL ($queryRawUnsafe/$executeRawUnsafe) in telework.service.ts or time-tracking.service.ts — all DB access via parameterized Prisma ORM
No hardcoded secrets, tokens, or passwords in any file in the two modules
All mutating routes on TeleworkSchedule (POST, PATCH :id, DELETE :id) have @RequirePermissions guard
All mutating routes on TimeEntry (POST, PATCH :id, DELETE :id) have @RequirePermissions guard
PATCH :id and DELETE :id on TimeEntry correctly use @UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard, OwnershipGuard) + @OwnershipCheck with bypassPermission
PATCH :id and DELETE :id on TeleworkSchedule use @RequirePermissions + @OwnershipCheck (OwnershipGuard is a global APP_GUARD in CommonModule)
GET /telework/:id has @OwnershipCheck + service-level IDOR check in findOne (telework.service.ts:392-400)
GET /time-tracking/:id has service-level IDOR check in findOne (time-tracking.service.ts:487-500) — correct
All route :id params use ParseUUIDPipe — no raw string-to-DB for entity PKs
taskId and projectId in CreateTimeEntryDto use @IsUUID() — correct
hours field in CreateTimeEntryDto has @Min(0.25) @Max(24) — correct range check
dayOfWeek in CreateRecurringRuleDto has @Min(0) @Max(6) — correct range check
No file upload endpoints in either module — no MIME/magic-byte surface
No external URL fields in either module — no SSRF surface
Global ValidationPipe in main.ts has whitelist:true + forbidNonWhitelisted:true — extra body fields are stripped
Global bodyLimit of 1 MiB set in main.ts FastifyAdapter config — prevents unbounded body attacks
PermissionsGuardV2 is registered as APP_GUARD in RbacModule and defaults to 'enforce' mode
OwnershipGuard is registered as APP_GUARD in CommonModule
ThrottlerBehindProxyGuard is a global APP_GUARD — telework and time-tracking inherit global rate limits
No JWT handling, cookie flags, or auth logic in either module
telework.service.ts findAll: non-readAll users are scoped to their own userId — no horizontal IDOR on list endpoint
time-tracking.service.ts findAll: strict ForbiddenException if userId filter targets another user without time_tracking:view_any permission
generateSchedulesFromRules: non-manage_any users are scoped to their own rules only
All Prisma queries use parameterized ORM calls — no SQL injection vector
